section.title: The Twelve Sent, the Five Thousand, and Peter's Confession
section.sectionRationale: This movement brings the Galilean ministry to its climax. Jesus sends the Twelve out with His own power and authority, which stirs Herod to wonder who Jesus is. Jesus then feeds five thousand with five loaves and two fish. On the far side of that abundance comes the turning point of Luke's Gospel: Peter confesses Jesus as the Christ of God, and Jesus at once foretells His suffering and calls all who would follow to take up the cross daily.
section.thesis: The question 'Who is this?' is answered here: Jesus is the Christ of God, who provides for His people out of nothing, yet whose messiahship runs through rejection, death, and resurrection, so that following Him means denying self and taking up the cross daily to save one's life by losing it for His sake.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the sending of the Twelve; Herod's perplexity over Jesus; the feeding of the five thousand; Peter's confession that Jesus is the Christ of God; and the first passion prediction with the call to take up the cross.

units.label: The Twelve sent out
units.note: power and authority to preach and heal

verses.text: Then Jesus called the Twelve together and gave them {{dynamis|power}} and authority over all demons, and power to cure diseases.
And He sent them out to proclaim the {{basileia|kingdom}} of God and to heal the sick.
“Take nothing for the journey,” He told them, “no staff, no bag, no bread, no money, no second tunic.
Whatever house you enter, stay there until you leave that area.
If anyone does not welcome you, shake the dust off your feet when you leave that town, as a testimony against them.”
So they set out and went from village to village, preaching the gospel and healing people everywhere.

units.label: Herod's perplexity
units.note: who is this man I hear such things about

verses.text: When Herod the tetrarch heard about all that was happening, he was perplexed. For some were saying that John had risen from the dead,
others that Elijah had appeared, and still others that a prophet of old had arisen.
“I beheaded John,” Herod said, “but who is this man I hear such things about?” And he kept trying to see Jesus.

units.label: The feeding of the five thousand
units.note: you give them something to eat

verses.text: Then the apostles returned and reported to Jesus all that they had done. Taking them away privately, He withdrew to a town called Bethsaida.
But the crowds found out and followed Him. He welcomed them and spoke to them about the kingdom of God, and He healed those who needed healing.

As the day neared its end, the Twelve came to Jesus and said, “Dismiss the crowd so they can go to the surrounding villages and countryside for lodging and provisions. For we are in a desolate place here.”
But Jesus told them, “You give them something to eat.” “We have only five loaves of bread and two fish,” they answered, “unless we go and buy food for all these people.”
(There were about five thousand men.) He told His disciples, “Have them sit down in groups of about fifty each.”
They did so, and everyone was seated.
Taking the five loaves and the two fish and looking up to heaven, Jesus spoke a blessing and broke them. Then He gave them to the disciples to set before the people.
They all ate and were satisfied, and the disciples picked up twelve basketfuls of broken pieces that were left over.
One day as Jesus was praying in private and the disciples were with Him, He questioned them: “Who do the crowds say I am?”
They replied, “Some say John the Baptist; others say Elijah; and still others, that a prophet of old has arisen.”
“But what about you?” Jesus asked. “Who do you say I am?” Peter answered, “The {{christos|Christ}} of God.”

units.label: The passion foretold and the call to the cross
units.note: deny yourself, take up your cross daily

verses.text: Jesus strictly warned them not to tell this to anyone.
“The Son of Man must suffer many things,” He said. “He must be rejected by the elders, chief priests, and scribes, and He must be killed and on the third day be raised to life.”
Then Jesus said to all of them, “If anyone wants to come after Me, he must deny himself and take up his {{stauros|cross}} daily and follow Me.
For whoever wants to save his {{psyche|life}} will lose it, but whoever loses his life for My sake will save it.
What does it profit a man to gain the whole world, yet lose or forfeit his very self?
If anyone is ashamed of Me and My words, the Son of Man will be ashamed of him when He comes in His glory and in the glory of the Father and of the holy angels.
But I tell you truly, some who are standing here will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God.”

dynamis.gloss: Power, might, effective force to accomplish something. Jesus gives the Twelve His own power over demons and disease, so that their mission carries not their strength but His. The same divine power fed the crowd and healed the sick; the apostles go out as bearers of a power that is entirely His gift.

basileia.gloss: Kingdom, the reign of God breaking into the world. The Twelve are sent to proclaim it, Jesus teaches the crowd about it, and He promises that some will see it come with power. The kingdom advances through preaching, healing, and provision, and supremely through the suffering and vindication of the King Himself.

christos.gloss: Christ, the Anointed One, the Greek for Messiah. It names the long-awaited King and Deliverer promised through the prophets. Peter's confession, 'The Christ of God,' is the turning point of the Gospel, yet Jesus immediately redefines what that title means: not immediate triumph, but a Messiah who must suffer, be killed, and rise.

stauros.gloss: Cross, the Roman instrument of execution carried by the condemned to the place of death. To take up one's cross daily is to embrace death to self and readiness to suffer for Jesus. The image is shocking on Jesus' lips before His own crucifixion, marking discipleship as a daily following of the crucified Christ.

psyche.gloss: Life, soul, the very self. Jesus turns the word on its edge: whoever grasps to save his life will lose it, but whoever loses it for His sake will save it. To clutch at self is to forfeit the true self; to surrender it to Christ is to find it. The whole person is at stake in following Him.

basic.context: Jesus gives the Twelve His own power and authority and sends them out to preach the kingdom and heal, traveling light and depending on God. Their mission spreads Jesus' fame so widely that Herod, who had beheaded John, is unsettled and keeps trying to see Him. When the apostles return, Jesus withdraws, but the crowds follow, and He feeds five thousand men from five loaves and two fish, with twelve baskets left over. Then, after praying alone, He asks the disciples who people say He is, and who they say He is. Peter answers, 'The Christ of God.' At once Jesus tells them that this Christ must suffer, be killed, and rise, and that anyone who would follow Him must deny himself and take up the cross daily.
basic.hermeneutics: The feeding and the confession belong together: Jesus provides abundantly, but His true identity is only rightly grasped when joined to His coming suffering. Peter's confession is correct, yet Jesus immediately fills the title 'Christ' with the cross, correcting every expectation of a triumphant Messiah without suffering. The call to take up the cross daily is the pattern of discipleship, not an optional extra.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus gives the Twelve His own dynamis, power, and sends them to proclaim the basileia, the kingdom. Peter confesses Him as the christos, the Anointed One of God, and Jesus at once speaks of the stauros, the cross: His own, and the one every follower must take up daily, losing his psyche, his life, to save it.
basic.typology: Jesus feeding the multitude in a desolate place recalls God giving manna in the wilderness and Elisha feeding a hundred with a little, marking Jesus as the true bread who provides for His people. The Son of Man who must suffer and rise fulfills the pattern of the rejected yet vindicated servant, and the twelve baskets echo the twelve tribes gathered around Him.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Christ of God, Peter's confession the hinge of the Gospel. But He is a Messiah who must suffer, be rejected, be killed, and rise on the third day. He provides bread for thousands and yet walks deliberately toward His own death, and He calls His people to follow the crucified King by dying to themselves. He is both the giver of life and the one who lays down His life.
basic.god: God's kingdom advances through Jesus and through the apostles He empowers, and it is God's own power at work in their mission and in the feeding. God's plan for the Christ runs through suffering, not around it, and it is a 'must,' a divine necessity. What God asks of disciples is wholehearted, self-denying allegiance to His Son.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 16 tells of manna in the wilderness, and 2 Kings 4 of Elisha feeding a hundred. Isaiah 53 foretells the suffering servant rejected and killed. And Luke 24 shows the risen Christ opening the Scriptures on how the Messiah had to suffer and enter His glory.
basic.oneStory: The long question of who Jesus is reaches its answer: He is the Christ of God, who feeds His people out of nothing and reigns as King. Yet His crown comes by a cross, for the Messiah must suffer and rise. The story now turns toward Jerusalem, and all who would share His kingdom are called to follow Him by daily taking up the cross.

context.literary: This is the climax and turning point of Luke's Galilean section. The repeated question 'Who is this?', raised by Herod and by the crowds, is finally answered by Peter, and from this point the Gospel leans toward the passion. The feeding of the five thousand, the only miracle in all four Gospels, sits just before the confession, and Jesus' first passion prediction and call to the cross immediately follow it.
context.historical: Herod Antipas, tetrarch of Galilee, had executed John the Baptist and now feared Jesus might be John raised. The disciples' instruction to travel without provisions reflected the urgency of the mission and dependence on hospitality. Bethsaida lay on the lake's northeast shore; the crowd of five thousand men, plus women and children, in a desolate place made the disciples' helplessness plain. Crucifixion was a familiar Roman horror, so 'take up your cross' would have landed with brutal force.
context.canonical: Peter's confession is the pivot of the Synoptic Gospels, echoed in Matthew 16 and Mark 8. The passion prediction begins a series that runs to the cross and resurrection. The feeding anticipates the messianic banquet and, for many readers, foreshadows the Lord's Supper, while the call to daily cross-bearing shapes the New Testament's whole vision of discipleship.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To answer the question of Jesus' identity with Peter's confession, to redefine that identity at once through the necessity of the cross and resurrection, and to summon every disciple to follow the suffering Christ by self-denial and daily cross-bearing, losing life in order to save it.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The sending of the Twelve and the feeding describe particular events that reveal Jesus' power and identity. The call in verses 23 to 26 is prescriptive for all disciples: 'if anyone' would come after Him, self-denial and daily cross-bearing are the abiding pattern, not a special calling for a few.

debates.question: Why did Jesus tell the disciples not to make His messiahship known?

views.label: To prevent a false, political messianism
views.case: Popular hopes expected a Messiah who would overthrow Rome in triumph; had the crowds proclaimed Jesus 'Christ' with that meaning, it would have ignited a movement opposite to His mission, so He silences it until the cross reveals what kind of Christ He is.

views.label: Because the confession was incomplete until the cross
views.case: The title 'Christ' is true but only rightly understood in light of His suffering and resurrection; Jesus withholds public proclamation until the events that give the title its true content have taken place.

debates.bearing: Either way Jesus insists that His messiahship be defined by the cross, guarding the confession from a triumphalism that skips the suffering He came to bear.

debates.question: What did Jesus mean that some standing there would see the kingdom of God before they die?

views.label: The transfiguration and resurrection glory
views.case: Within days some of them see Jesus transfigured, a glimpse of the kingdom's glory, and soon after His resurrection and the coming of the Spirit display the kingdom's arrival with power, all within their lifetime.

views.label: The whole redemptive breaking-in of the kingdom
views.case: Others read it broadly of the kingdom's advance through Jesus' death, resurrection, ascension, and the birth of the church, all of which those present would live to witness.

debates.bearing: Both keep the promise within the hearers' lifetime and tie the kingdom's coming to the events of Jesus' death, resurrection, and the Spirit rather than to a distant future only.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus shares His own dynamis, power, with the Twelve and sends them to proclaim the basileia, the kingdom of God, which He also displays by feeding thousands from almost nothing. Then, after praying alone, He draws out Peter's confession: 'The christos of God,' the Anointed King the prophets promised. But the moment the title is spoken, Jesus fills it with the stauros, the cross. The Son of Man must suffer, be killed, and rise, and anyone who would follow must take up his own cross daily. He presses the paradox with the word psyche, life or self: grasp to save your life and you lose it; lose it for His sake and you save it. To find the true self, the self must be surrendered to Christ.

typology.type: Feeding the multitude in the wilderness
typology.fulfillment: Jesus is the true bread who provides for His people, as God gave manna, Exodus 16, and as He fulfills in John 6 declaring Himself the bread of life.

typology.type: The Son of Man who must suffer and rise
typology.fulfillment: Jesus fulfills the suffering servant, rejected, killed, and vindicated, Isaiah 53:3-12, and the exalted Son of Man of Daniel 7:13-14.
The abundance gathered by the Twelve echoes the twelve tribes gathered around the Messiah, the people God provides for in fullness.

study.christ: Peter's confession is the mountain peak of the Gospel so far: Jesus is the Christ of God, the promised King. Yet Jesus will not let the title float free of the cross. In the same breath He declares that the Son of Man must suffer, be rejected by Israel's leaders, be killed, and on the third day rise. He is a Messiah crowned through crucifixion. He feeds five thousand to show He is the giver of life, and He turns toward Jerusalem to give His own life. And He calls His followers to know Him truly by following the crucified King, denying themselves and taking up the cross daily, for to be ashamed of Him now is to be disowned when He comes in glory.
study.god: God's kingdom breaks in through Jesus and through the apostles He fills with His power, and it is God who satisfies the hungry crowd from a boy's small lunch. The plan of God for His Christ runs straight through suffering: the Son of Man 'must' suffer and be killed and rise, a divine necessity, not a tragic accident. And the God who gives His Son asks in return a whole-life allegiance, a willingness to lose one's life for Christ's sake in order truly to find it, with the sure hope of His coming glory.

crossReferences.note: God feeds Israel with manna in the wilderness, the pattern behind Jesus feeding the multitude in a desolate place.
The servant despised, rejected, and killed for others, yet vindicated, which Jesus' passion prediction fulfills.
The Son of Man given everlasting dominion, the glory in which Jesus says He will come.
The risen Christ teaches that the Messiah had to suffer these things and then enter His glory, confirming the necessity Jesus announces here.

study.oneStory: The question that has run through the Gospel, 'Who is this?', is finally answered: Jesus is the Christ of God, who feeds His people out of nothing and reigns as the promised King. But His throne is reached by a cross, for the Messiah must suffer, be killed, and rise. From here the story turns toward Jerusalem, and the King calls all who would share His kingdom to follow Him by daily taking up the cross, losing their lives to find them, in the certain hope of the glory in which He will come.

quiz.q: What did Jesus give the Twelve when He sent them out?

options.text: His own power and authority over demons and disease, to preach the kingdom and heal.
options.note: Luke 9:1-2 shows Jesus entrusting them with His power for their mission.

options.text: Detailed instructions to travel light and depend on hospitality.
options.note: He did give such instructions, but the heart of the sending was the power and authority He gave them.

options.text: Money and provisions for a long journey.
options.note: The opposite; He told them to take nothing for the journey.

options.text: Authority to rule over the towns they entered.
options.note: They were sent to serve, preach, and heal, not to rule over towns.

quiz.q: What happened when Jesus fed the five thousand?

options.text: He multiplied five loaves and two fish so all ate and were satisfied, with twelve baskets left over.
options.note: Luke 9:16-17 records the abundant provision from almost nothing.

options.text: He sent the crowd to buy food in the surrounding villages.
options.note: That was the disciples' suggestion, which Jesus overruled by providing Himself.

options.text: He told the disciples there was nothing they could do.
options.note: He said 'You give them something to eat,' then provided through them; He did not counsel helplessness.

options.text: He fed only the Twelve, not the crowd.
options.note: The whole crowd of about five thousand men ate and were satisfied.

quiz.q: How did Peter answer when Jesus asked, 'Who do you say I am?'

options.text: The Christ of God, the promised Anointed King.
options.note: Luke 9:20 gives Peter's confession, the turning point of the Gospel.

options.text: A great prophet like Elijah or one of the prophets of old.
options.note: That was what the crowds said; Peter's answer went further, confessing Him as the Christ.

options.text: John the Baptist raised from the dead.
options.note: This was a popular guess, even Herod's fear, not Peter's confession.

options.text: A teacher sent to reform Israel's religion.
options.note: Peter confessed far more: Jesus is the Christ of God.

quiz.q: What did Jesus teach about His messiahship right after Peter's confession?

options.text: That the Son of Man must suffer, be rejected, be killed, and on the third day rise.
options.note: Luke 9:22 redefines the Christ through the necessity of the cross and resurrection.

options.text: That He would soon be crowned and honored by the people.
options.note: He will be glorified, but only through suffering and death first, which Jesus stressed here.

options.text: That He would overthrow the Romans and restore Israel's kingdom.
options.note: This was the popular hope Jesus deliberately corrected by pointing to the cross.

options.text: That His identity should now be proclaimed openly everywhere.
options.note: He strictly warned them not to tell anyone yet, until the cross gave the title its meaning.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say it means to follow Him?

options.text: To deny yourself, take up your cross daily, and lose your life for His sake in order to save it.
options.note: Luke 9:23-24 sets out self-denial and daily cross-bearing as the pattern of discipleship.

options.text: To be willing to endure hardship when it happens to come.
options.note: Following Him does mean hardship, but Jesus calls for deliberate, daily self-denial, not merely passive endurance.

options.text: To admire His teaching while keeping your life as it is.
options.note: Jesus calls for losing your life for His sake, not admiration that changes nothing.

options.text: To gain the whole world and secure your own comfort.
options.note: Jesus warns that gaining the world while forfeiting your self profits nothing.

questions: Jesus sent the Twelve out depending on Him rather than on their own resources. Where is He calling you to step out in trust rather than waiting until you feel fully equipped?
The disciples saw only five loaves and two fish; Jesus saw enough to satisfy thousands. What impossible-looking need are you tempted to measure by your resources instead of bringing to Him?
Jesus asked, 'Who do you say I am?' It is not enough to know what others say. How would you answer that question today, and does your life reflect that answer?
Peter confessed the Christ, but Jesus insisted the Christ must suffer. Where are you tempted to want the glory of following Jesus without the cost of the cross?
Jesus says whoever loses his life for His sake will save it. What might it look like this week to lose your life for Him in some concrete, costly way?

translationNotes.note: 'The Christ of God' renders the Greek for the Anointed One, the Messiah, belonging to God; Matthew adds 'the Son of the living God,' but Luke's shorter form carries the same confession.
The word 'daily' is distinctive to Luke's account of this saying, stressing that cross-bearing is not a single heroic act but the ongoing shape of following Jesus.
The word rendered 'life' is psyche, which also means soul or self; the saying trades on both senses, that grasping to keep one's life forfeits the true self.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 9:1-27 only (verses 28-62 belong to the next study). Headings, line breaks, footnote and poem markers were skipped when building verse text. No em or en dashes occur in this passage; none remain. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon on Luke 9:1-27 specifically was verified this session (the closest verified messages were on the Markan and Matthean parallels).
